article.title: OpenBSD 7.9 Marks the Project's 60th Release With a Heterogeneous CPU Scheduler, WiFi 6, and a Post-Quantum TLS Upgrade
article.summary: OpenBSD 7.9 is the project's 60th release, adding a heterogeneous CPU scheduler, WiFi 6, LibreSSL 4.3.0 with post-quantum TLS, a 255-core amd64 ceiling, delayed hibernation, and Apple Virtualization support.
article.body_markdown:
## Overview

OpenBSD 7.9 shipped on May 19, 2026, landing on its regular six-month schedule as the project's 60th release, according to the [official release page](https://www.openbsd.org/79.html). The release continues OpenBSD's unbroken cadence with improvements spread across the kernel, networking stack, platform support, cryptography, and virtualization. [Linuxiac](https://linuxiac.com/openbsd-7-9-brings-delayed-hibernation-and-graphics-driver-updates/) described it as arriving on schedule with a broad set of changes including CPU scheduling improvements and delayed hibernation.

## CPU Scheduler Gains Heterogeneous Core Awareness

One of the most architecturally significant additions is a new kernel mechanism for managing CPU cores with different speeds, as detailed on the [OpenBSD release page](https://www.openbsd.org/79.html). Modern ARM chips—and increasingly x86 designs—pair fast performance cores with slower efficiency cores, and until this release OpenBSD's scheduler had no way to steer workloads away from specific core classes at runtime. The new `hw.blockcpu` sysctl allows administrators to exclude SMT threads, efficient cores, or slower cores from scheduling on amd64 and arm64 systems, according to [Linuxiac](https://linuxiac.com/openbsd-7-9-brings-delayed-hibernation-and-graphics-driver-updates/).

The same release also replaces the kernel's compare-and-swap spinlock mechanism in mutexes with a parking lock design, as documented by [Linuxiac](https://linuxiac.com/openbsd-7-9-brings-delayed-hibernation-and-graphics-driver-updates/). Parallel fault handling is now enabled on both amd64 and arm64.

## amd64 Raises Its CPU Ceiling to 255

On the amd64 platform, the maximum supported CPU count rises to 255, as noted by the [OpenBSD project](https://www.openbsd.org/79.html) and confirmed by [Linuxiac](https://linuxiac.com/openbsd-7-9-brings-delayed-hibernation-and-graphics-driver-updates/). The release also adds System Management Unit (SMU) support to the `amdpmc(4)` driver, enabling lower power consumption in suspend mode on AMD processors, and fixes floating-point state leakage on AMD Zen and Zen+ systems. Systems with more than 512 GB of RAM receive fixes for DM page-table entry issues.

## Wireless: 802.11ax and Protected Management Frames

OpenBSD 7.9 adds basic 802.11ax (WiFi 6) support with 160 MHz channel width at 5 GHz, and WiFi 6E radio support for BZ devices, as listed on the [official release page](https://www.openbsd.org/79.html). Protected Management Frames are now supported across the `iwm(4)`, `iwx(4)`, and `qwx(4)` drivers. Power-save mode is enabled by default for the `iwx(4)` driver, according to [Linuxiac](https://linuxiac.com/openbsd-7-9-brings-delayed-hibernation-and-graphics-driver-updates/).

## LibreSSL 4.3.0 Brings Post-Quantum TLS Key Exchange

The release ships LibreSSL 4.3.0, which adds ML-KEM768_X25519 hybrid key exchange support in TLS, according to the [OpenBSD project](https://www.openbsd.org/79.html). Combining ML-KEM with the classical X25519 key exchange provides a hybrid mode intended to guard against both classical and quantum adversaries. LibreSSL 4.3.0 also fixes an X.509 verifier depth-checking off-by-one error that could have enabled a heap overwrite.

OpenSSH 10.3 is also included, with fixes for shell metacharacter validation in usernames, authorized-keys principal matching, and setuid/setgid bit handling in the legacy `scp(1)` mode, as documented on the [OpenBSD release page](https://www.openbsd.org/79.html).

## Delayed Hibernation and Power Management

A new delayed hibernation feature lets suspended systems automatically hibernate after a configurable interval, configured via the `machdep.hibernatedelay` sysctl. [LWN.net](https://lwn.net/Articles/1073933/) summarizes this as "the ability to hibernate a suspended system after a configurable delay." The feature addresses the familiar laptop scenario where a suspended system slowly drains its battery overnight because it never transitioned from sleep to hibernate.

## Networking: VLAN-Aware Bridges, IPv6 by Default, and pf Improvements

The `veb(4)` virtual Ethernet bridge driver becomes VLAN-aware, gaining port VLAN identifiers (PVID), access/trunk/hybrid port modes, and private VLAN support per RFC 5517, per the [OpenBSD release page](https://www.openbsd.org/79.html). IPv6 SLAAC (Stateless Address Autoconfiguration) is now enabled by default. The `pf(4)` packet filter gains source and state limiters. Socket splicing has been unlocked for improved multi-processor performance.

A security fix prevents root from bypassing the effects of `bpf(4)` BIOCLOCK, closing a privilege-escalation path in the Berkeley Packet Filter, as documented by both [OpenBSD](https://www.openbsd.org/79.html) and [Heise Online](https://www.heise.de/en/news/OpenBSD-7-9-Mythos-bug-fixed-more-partitions-and-security-11300769.html).

## Partition Table Expansion and an AI-Found Bug Already Patched

The disklabel architecture has been extended to support up to 52 partitions per disk, up from the previous limit of 16, according to [Heise Online](https://www.heise.de/en/news/OpenBSD-7-9-Mythos-bug-fixed-more-partitions-and-security-11300769.html). The expansion works by widening the internal partition coding from 4 to 6 bits, which allows up to 64 theoretical partitions; the 52-partition practical limit reflects OpenBSD's use of alphanumeric characters for partition labels.

Heise also reported on a 27-year-old TCP SACK vulnerability in OpenBSD that was identified by an AI tool. According to [Heise Online](https://www.heise.de/en/news/OpenBSD-7-9-Mythos-bug-fixed-more-partitions-and-security-11300769.html), the development team had already patched the flaw in OpenBSD 7.8 using three lines of code before any public announcement. The vulnerability involved an integer overflow when processing TCP gaps where sequence identifiers differed by 2^31 bytes, potentially causing a kernel crash as a denial-of-service condition; the article noted the exploit was "likely never occurred in practice."

## Virtualization and Platform Additions

OpenBSD 7.9 can now run as a guest under Apple Virtualization on M-series Macs, as noted on the [official release page](https://www.openbsd.org/79.html). A new `vmboot` tiny kernel enables in-place upgrades via `sysupgrade(8)` on `vmd(8)` virtual machines. AMD SEV confidential computing support is extended to CD and vioscsi virtual devices.

Platform additions include RK3588/RK3576 SoC support and an ice(4) networking driver on arm64, and full SpacemiT K1 SoC support on riscv64 with clock, GPIO, PCIe, and Ethernet drivers. The luna88k platform switches to GCC 4 with Position Independent Executables enabled by default.

USB4 controller support arrives via the new `nhi(4)` driver. The DRM graphics subsystem is updated to align with Linux kernel 6.18.22.

## Routing Daemon and Package Updates

The `bgpd(8)` routing daemon received a significant internal rewrite: its Adj-RIB-Out handling was redesigned to reduce memory usage by more than 50 percent, and filter performance improved by 25 percent, according to the [OpenBSD project](https://www.openbsd.org/79.html). The release ships 13,044 pre-built packages for amd64 and 12,883 for aarch64.

## What We Don't Know

The 7.9 release notes do not include a comprehensive user-facing upgrade guide for the heterogeneous CPU scheduler changes, so the extent to which existing workloads on mixed-core hardware will benefit without manual `hw.blockcpu` tuning is unclear. The practical impact of the 52-partition expansion—whether distribution tooling will expose it—depends on downstream packaging decisions outside the OpenBSD project's control.
